policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• All pharmacies must submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review
(Pro-DUR) program using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction
format. NCPDP format specifications can be found in the MEVS Manual available at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/5010/MEVS/MEVS_DVS_Provider_Manual_(5010).pdf.
• All pharmacy claims are subject to Medicaid Pharmacy Program requirements, including PA and criteria
requirements.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx
the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, and Medicaid
Update articles. All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor)
must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost. Providers should refer to the
NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, for requirements
when NYS Medicaid is secondary payor. Claim level identifiers are required on eligible 340B purchased drug
claims for NYS Medicaid members.
Through monitoring real-time claims, NYRx has identified two trends in pharmacy submitted 340B
drug claims:
1.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_1.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_1.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_1.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• All pharmacies must submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review
(Pro-DUR) program using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction
format. NCPDP format specifications can be found in the MEVS Manual available at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/5010/MEVS/MEVS_DVS_Provider_Manual_(5010).pdf.
• All pharmacy claims are subject to Medicaid Pharmacy Program requirements, including PA and criteria
requirements.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx
the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, and Medicaid
Update articles. All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor)
must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost. Providers should refer to the
NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, for requirements
when NYS Medicaid is secondary payor. Claim level identifiers are required on eligible 340B purchased drug
claims for NYS Medicaid members.
Through monitoring real-time claims, NYRx has identified two trends in pharmacy submitted 340B
drug claims:
1.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

{
  "headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, and the most recent policy source dated February 1, 2024, dual eligible pharmacy claims are subject to Medicaid Pharmacy Program requirements, including PA and criteria requirements. The provider manual provides more up-to-date guidance on Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid.",
  "policy_definition": "[New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8] — [Chunk ID: mu_no8_apr23_pr.pdf::0021] — [2023-04-01]-[pages: [9]] - Pharmacy claims are subject to Medicaid requirements, including PA and criteria requirements.",
  "provider_manual_definition": "[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] — [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0082] — [2025-10-01]-[pages: [21]] - The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations.",
  "similarities": ["Similarity: Pharmacy claims are subject to Medicaid requirements.", "[New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8] — [Chunk ID: mu_no8_apr23_pr.pdf::0021] — [2023-04-01]-[pages: [9]]"],
  "differences": ["Difference: The provider manual provides more up-to-date guidance on Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid.", "[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] — [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0082] — [2025-10-01]-[pages: [21]]"],

test_id_3.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_3.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_3.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”

test_id_5.answer:
{
  "headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, dual eligible pharmacy claims are subject to Medicaid Pharmacy Program requirements, including PA and criteria requirements. The provider manual provides more up-to-date guidance on Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid.",
  "policy_definition": "[New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8] — [Chunk ID: mu_no8_apr23_pr.pdf::0021] — [2023-04-01]-[pages: [9]] - Pharmacy claims are subject to Medicaid requirements, including PA and criteria requirements.",
  "provider_manual_definition": "[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] — [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0082]-[Effective Date: 2025-10-01]-[pages: [21]] - The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations.",
  "similarities": ["Similarity: Pharmacy claims are subject to Medicaid requirements.", "[New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8] — [Chunk ID: mu_no8_apr23_pr.pdf::0021] — [2023-04-01]-[pages: [9]]"],
  "differences": ["Difference: The provider manual provides more up-to-date guidance on Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid.", "[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] — [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0082]-[Effective Date: 2025-10-01]-[pages: [21]]"],
  "caveats": "None"
}
test_id_5.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_5.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_5.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_6.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_6.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

compare_sections.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, and the most recent policy source dated April 1, 2025, primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims is that the pharmacy is responsible for billing timely and to the appropriate party. The provider manual provides more up-to-date guidance on this topic.

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] — [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0037]-[Effective Date: 2025-10-01]-[pages: [11]]

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The information in this article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• If date of notification of eligibility is after 90 days of DOS, LTC/FC facility pharmacies may send the claim
information for retro-PA consideration, within 30 days from the date of notification of eligibility, to the
Medicaid Pharmacy unit at PPNO@health.ny.gov. All claims submitted for PA after 30 days of
eligibility determination will be denied. Additional information on submitting claims over 90 from DOS
June 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/QuickRefDocs/FOD-7001_Sub_Claims_Over_90_
days_Old.pdf.
• Any claim submitted with a DOS after the date of notification of eligibility will be denied. The claim should
have been billed on the DOS.
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility as well as the pharmacy, to actively and
regularly seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their members.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, which may include the facility,
commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Additional information regarding timely billing can be found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.

must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• If date of notification of eligibility is after 90 days of DOS, LTC/FC facility pharmacies may send the claim
information for retro-PA consideration, within 30 days from the date of notification of eligibility, to the
Medicaid Pharmacy unit at PPNO@health.ny.gov. All claims submitted for PA after 30 days of
eligibility determination will be denied. Additional information on submitting claims over 90 from DOS
June 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/QuickRefDocs/FOD-7001_Sub_Claims_Over_90_
days_Old.pdf.
• Any claim submitted with a DOS after the date of notification of eligibility will be denied. The claim should
have been billed on the DOS.
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility as well as the pharmacy, to actively and
regularly seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their members.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, which may include the facility,
commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Additional information regarding timely billing can be found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Incentive payments are not applicable to services provided to dual-eligible members (members in receipt of
both Medicare and NYS Medicaid), NYS Medicaid members with third-party health insurance (TPHI), or
individuals with the Essential Plan (EP), Qualified Health Plan (QHP), Managed Long-Term Care (MLTC)
Plan, and Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2024 Volume 40 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medical and Surgical Supplies
Reimbursement for each covered medical/surgical supply will be the lower of:
• The price as indicated on the New York State List of Medical/Surgical Supplies, or
• The usual and customary price charged to the general public.
"Covered supplies" are those on the OTC and Supply Fee Schedule manual located here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/index.aspx. For supplies not on this list, only those supplies for
which the prescriber has obtained prior approval are covered.
Co-payments for Drugs and Medical Supplies
The New York State Medicaid Program charges co-payments for many drug and medical supply items.
Health care providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of a Medicaid member's ability to
pay co-payments:
• Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to
pay the co-payment, per Social Security Act (SSA) § 1916 and Title 18 NYCRR § 360-7.12.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_11.answer:
{
  "headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims is that the pharmacy bills timely and to the appropriate party. The policy guidance from April 1, 2024, states that pharmacies are responsible for billing timely and to the appropriate party, but does not explicitly address primary payer responsibility. The provider manual provides more up-to-date guidance on this topic, which should receive more weight by default.",
  "policy_definition": "[New York State Medicaid Update October 2024 Volume 40 Number 11] — [Chunk ID: mu_no11_oct24_pr.pdf::0044]-[Effective Date: 2024-10-01]-[pages: [12]] - Pharmacies are responsible for billing timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.",
  "provider_manual_definition": "[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] — [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070]-[Effective Date: 2025-10-01]-[pages: [18, 19]] - It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.",
  "similarities": ["Pharmacy claims are subject to timely billing requirements.", "[New York State Medicaid Update October 2024 Volume 40 Number 11] — [Chunk ID: mu_no11_oct24_pr.pdf::0044]-[Effective Date: 2024-10-01]-[pages: [12]]"],
  "differences": ["Policy guidance does not explicitly address co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members.", "[New York State Medicaid Update October 2024 Volume 40 Number 11] — [Chunk ID: mu_no11_oct24_pr.pdf::0044]-[Effective Date: 2024-10-01]-[pages: [12]]"],
  "caveats": "None"
}
test_id_11.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_11.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_11.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_12.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

compare_sections.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims is that the pharmacy bills timely and to the appropriate party. The policy guidance from May 2024 states that pharmacies are responsible for billing timely and to the appropriate party, but does not explicitly address co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members. The provider manual provides more up-to-date guidance on this topic, which should receive more weight by default.

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
For dual-eligible members (in receipt of both Medicare and NYS Medicaid) who have enrolled in the hospice
program, NYS DOH will use a file supplied by Medicare to load the RR/E code C2 in eMedNY on the members
NYS Medicaid record.
For non-dual NYS Medicaid members, RR/E code C2 will be manually added to the record of the NYS
Medicaid member by NYS DOH upon receipt of the Hospice Care Recipient Restriction/Exception (RR/E)
Code Update Form, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/redesign/mrt90/mltc
_policy/2023/docs/att1.pdf, from an LDSS, MMCO, or hospice provider. If a NYS Medicaid member in
receipt of hospice services elects to discontinue hospice services, RR/E code C2 must be manually
closed by NYS DOH.
Due to limitations of data received from Medicare, NYS Medicaid members no longer in receipt of hospice
care will need to have RR/E code C2 manually closed with a manually entered end-date.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Refusing to
provide services is an unacceptable practice.
o Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the
copayment each time the member is provided with services or goods, sending bills or any other
legal means.
• Pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is
collected from a Medicaid member. Each claim that requires a co-payment will have the co-payment
automatically deducted from the final payment when the claim is approved for payment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
For dual-eligible members (in receipt of both Medicare and NYS Medicaid) who have enrolled in the hospice
program, NYS DOH will use a file supplied by Medicare to load the RR/E code C2 in eMedNY on the members
NYS Medicaid record.
For non-dual NYS Medicaid members, RR/E code C2 will be manually added to the record of the NYS
Medicaid member by NYS DOH upon receipt of the Hospice Care Recipient Restriction/Exception (RR/E)
Code Update Form, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/redesign/mrt90/mltc
_policy/2023/docs/att1.pdf, from an LDSS, MMCO, or hospice provider. If a NYS Medicaid member in
receipt of hospice services elects to discontinue hospice services, RR/E code C2 must be manually
closed by NYS DOH.
Due to limitations of data received from Medicare, NYS Medicaid members no longer in receipt of hospice
care will need to have RR/E code C2 manually closed with a manually entered end-date.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medical and Surgical Supplies
Reimbursement for each covered medical/surgical supply will be the lower of:
• The price as indicated on the New York State List of Medical/Surgical Supplies, or
• The usual and customary price charged to the general public.
"Covered supplies" are those on the OTC and Supply Fee Schedule manual located here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/index.aspx. For supplies not on this list, only those supplies for
which the prescriber has obtained prior approval are covered.
Co-payments for Drugs and Medical Supplies
The New York State Medicaid Program charges co-payments for many drug and medical supply items.
Health care providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of a Medicaid member's ability to
pay co-payments:
• Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to
pay the co-payment, per Social Security Act (SSA) § 1916 and Title 18 NYCRR § 360-7.12.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Refusing to
provide services is an unacceptable practice.
o Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the
copayment each time the member is provided with services or goods, sending bills or any other
legal means.
• Pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is
collected from a Medicaid member. Each claim that requires a co-payment will have the co-payment
automatically deducted from the final payment when the claim is approved for payment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_14.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."

It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_16.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

compare_sections.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, Medicaid is not responsible for billing Medicare Part D-excluded drugs. In contrast, the policy guidance from April 2025 states that Medicaid will cover certain prescription drugs not covered by Medicare Part D, but this is not explicitly stated as a co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members.
compare_sections.policy_definition: [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4] — [Chunk ID: mu_no04_apr25_pr.pdf::0026]-[Effective Date: 2025-04-01]-[pages: [8]] - Certain services may be rejected by the primary payer, but coverage should still be pursued in situations including, but not limited to: • prior authorization (PA) needs to be obtained from the primary payer; • billing Medicare Part D for services covered by Medicare Part B, or billing Medicare Part B for services covered by Medicare Part D and getting a rejection; • submitting to the wrong third-party causing claims to bypass incorrectly.
compare_sections.provider_manual_definition: [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0125]-[Effective Date: 2025-10-01]-[pages: [30, 31]] - All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care facilities may: • Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or • Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies.

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_17.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

compare_sections.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, Medicaid is not responsible for billing Medicare Part D-excluded drugs. The policy guidance from April 2025 states that Medicaid will cover certain prescription drugs not covered by Medicare Part D, but this does not explicitly address co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members. New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4 is the newest policy source dated 2025-04-01, while New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines is the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01. The provider manual provides more up-to-date guidance on handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs.

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
